one_line_takeaway: In a real hospital-scale IT org, involving USERS in vulnerability remediation was the big win; bolting an LLM onto the technicians helped most on complex vulns but backfired on simple SSL/SSH ones (slower, no satisfaction gain), making LLM support a task-complexity-dependent double-edged sword rather than a uniform speedup.
key_finding: User engagement enhancement is the workhorse: relative to the traditional process it strongly raised engagement (UEC Gue vs Gta p=0.000, d=1.342), cut remediation duration (RDC p=0.000, d=1.005), and improved satisfaction (information accuracy p=0.03/d=0.647, consistency p=0.04/d=0.623, assistance reliability p=0.032/d=0.651). Adding LLM support to technicians (Gllm) is genuinely double-edged and task-complexity-dependent: it further boosted engagement overall (Gllm vs Gue p=0.045, d=0.462) — significantly so for SIMPLE vulns (p=0.027, d=0.834) but not for complex ones (p=0.361, d=0.198) — yet it did NOT significantly cut duration versus Gue and even slightly increased it for simple vulns, while helping (non-significantly) on complex vulns. Critically, LLM support produced NO significant satisfaction gain over Gue and a slight (non-significant) decline. Study 3 explains why: LLM drafts are strong for mature/complex fixes (fast preliminary solutions, novel overlooked approaches) but for simple SSL/SSH vulns they are too generic, ignore per-environment compatibility, and are sometimes inexecutable, forcing extra technician verification; satisfaction dips partly because some users see remediation as outside their job. Report data: 26/29 (93.1%) of technician reports adopted the LLM's suggestion, but only 17/29 (58.6%) rated it >=70% effective, 9/29 (31%) rated 30-70%, and 3/29 (10.3%) rated <=30%.
core_contribution: A three-step, mixed-method field study (conducted inside one ~600-person biotechnology research institution over ~1 month in July-Aug 2023) that designs and empirically validates an LLM-supported COLLABORATIVE vulnerability-remediation process. The paper's own contribution is not a new model or detector; it is a socio-technical process redesign plus first-hand evidence of what LLMs can and cannot do when embedded into a real, multi-stakeholder cybersecurity operation. The redesign adds two components on top of the institution's existing remediation workflow: (1) 'User Engagement Enhancement' (alerts and remediation solutions are shared directly with users, who can then co-remediate with technicians), and (2) 'LLM-supported Technician Enhancement' (technicians get an LLM tool to draft/refine remediation plans; the LLM is deliberately NOT exposed to end users). The central empirical claim is that user engagement is the dominant driver of improvement, while adding LLM support to technicians is a genuine double-edged sword.
threat_model: This is a security-DOMAIN paper but NOT an adversarial threat-model paper, and it does not posit an attacker. The 'risk' it engages is the reliability/misinformation hazard of injecting a generative model into a high-stakes operational process: LLMs may produce persuasive but overly generic, idealistic, incomplete, or inexecutable remediation solutions that, if trusted, could lead to insufficient patching or unintended system breakage in a high-stakes setting. The paper studies how to safely embed the LLM as a technician-side assistant (kept behind human expert review, never given directly to non-expert users) rather than how to defend the LLM against attack. Do not read an attacker/defender frame into it.
limitations: Single-institution field study with tiny operator N (only 6 technicians total, 2 per condition) and 87 users, over just ~1 month — the authors flag possible carry-over effects between the three phases and that the window is short. No condition isolates LLM support WITHOUT user engagement (they deliberately omit LLM-only), so the pure marginal effect of the LLM on technicians cannot be cleanly separated from the engagement change it induces. The engagement metric is a coarse 3-level ordinal code and duration/complexity are self-reported by technicians in reports (self-report and sample bias acknowledged). The Complex/Simple split is coarse — complexity varies within each category. Personal characteristics (user age/gender, technician prior familiarity with specific vulns) and time-of-day cognitive fluctuations were uncontrolled. The domain (remediation) is one where LLM output can be verified in the short term; the authors caution the double-edged conclusions may not transfer to high-stakes tasks whose outcomes cannot be validated quickly. A specific proprietary tool (Monica/GPT-4, 2023) limits reproducibility and temporal generality.

datasets_used: Study 1 survey: ~600-person institution surveyed, 266 responses (44.33% rate), 246 valid after filtering (186 in experienced group Gw, 60 in inexperienced group Gw/o)
Study 2 field experiment: 29 real high-risk vulnerabilities (12 Complex MySQL/Apache+rare, 17 Simple SSL/SSH) across 87 users and 6 technicians in 3 conditions
Study 2 post-survey: 85 valid user satisfaction responses (28 Gta, 28 Gue, 29 Gllm)
Study 3: 29 technician remediation reports from the Gllm condition
Study 3: semi-structured interviews with 18 participants (6 technicians + 12 users; 2 technicians + 4 users per condition)
quantitative_results: Study1 lower satisfaction for experienced users (Gw): info timeliness p=0.006 d=0.330, accuracy p=0.009 d=0.249, consistency p=0.050 d=0.141; service responsiveness p=0.016 d=0.276; help-seeking willingness p=0.051 d=0.117, involvement willingness p=0.047 d=0.183

User engagement change: UEC(Gue,Gta) p=0.000 d=1.342; UEC(Gllm,Gta) p=0.000 d=2.072; UEC(Gllm,Gue) p=0.045 d=0.462; simple vulns UEC(Gllm,Gue) p=0.027 d=0.834; complex vulns p=0.361 d=0.198 (not significant)

Remediation duration change: RDC(Gue,Gta) p=0.000 d=1.005 (reduced); RDC(Gllm,Gta) p=0.099 d=0.346 (reduced); RDC(Gllm,Gue) increased but not significant; for simple vulns RDC(Gllm,Gta) became insignificant and RDC(Gllm,Gue) turned positive (LLM slowed simple fixes)

Satisfaction Gue vs Gta: information accuracy p=0.03 d=0.647, consistency p=0.04 d=0.623, assistance reliability p=0.032 d=0.651; Gue vs Gllm: no significant differences, slight (non-significant) decline on most metrics
LLM solution adoption in 29 Gllm reports: 26/29 (93.1%) adopted; effectiveness ratings: 17/29 (58.6%) >=70%, 9/29 (31%) in 30-70%, 3/29 (10.3%) <=30%
baselines_compared: Tradition condition Gta = existing institutional vulnerability-remediation process (the baseline)
User Engagement Enhancement condition Gue (users looped in, no LLM)
LLM-supported Technician Enhancement condition Gllm (Gue + LLM for technicians)
Gw (experienced) vs Gw/o (inexperienced) user groups in Study 1
LLM tools trialed before selection: ChatGPT (GPT-3.5), Chat8 (GPT-3.5), and Monica (GPT-4, chosen)
